author: @victoryfirst2878
text:
You have made a very important point about INSULATION Robert. When I used to work with HVAC system years ago. I was taught that INSULATION WAS THE FIRST THING YOU DID, PERIOD !! Then any type of heating method would work just fine and keep cost at bay. 
          Today most people choose to heat first and insulate second. Totally the wrong approach with the worst outcome. I added insulation to my dads house three years ago. That consisted of going from six inches of fiberglass to adding on top twelve inches more ultra thin continuous strand insulation on top of the six inches. This saved oil heating costs of 60 percent. That was a hell of a savings especially when the stove is a Dynotherm which is 93 percent efficient using K-1 Kerosine.  This really makes a difference with the cost of heating. PERIOD. This is a point that most people do not and will not understand....

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
When you have a massive shop, you just need a whole lot of insulation and, ultimately, a whole lot of heat power.

MY approach, however, is to ROB heat from the EARTH because THAT heat is FREE!

All I need to do here, since I am up  on the side of a very tall and steep hill, with a lot of water in it, is simply drill a very long hole somewhat sideways, into the hill, maybe a good 200 meters or so, and connect the outlet through a sealed concrete plug, to a radiator that will allow the water to only escape through that radiator.

From there, it can be used for other purposes, naturally, seeing as it is perfectly good 'well' water... its just that the well is, in this specific case, more-or-less horizontal, rather than vertical.

On top of that, seeing as there is a little bit of hydrostatic pressure going on, if I put a decent little sediment filter just downstream of the concrete plug, but yet up stream of the rest of the system, [and preferably with a valve to keep all the upstream water from needlessly all draining out into places and making a big mess], which can be cleaned or changed out on occasion, I can then put a small hydrostatic motor there which can turn a small fan, so that the water, while it is giving off its heat, is also powering the very fan that is making this more possible.

I plan on using a salvaged automotive radiator, since it is a very thermally efficient, and some times you can get them reasonably cheap, compared to a new one, and if you do not mind a few dings and some work straightening out all of the fins, you can usually get them readily enough from cars which have bee wrecked. Just bring along some water and maybe some stoppers so you can fill them and see if they leak before you buy them.

If you are a master at soldering, some times you can carefully patch up those leaks, although there is a logical limit to how big of a hole you can patch, you may in some cases find it easier to crimp off a couple of leaky tubes and solder their end shut, than to try to plug the holes in them. It all depends on how big the hole is, and how many holes there are.

Naturally try to pick ones which do not leak over ones which leak. 

It is TRULY [minus the initial cost of the set up anyhow] FREE energy!

Will it work for EVERY application?

No.

But when you think about it, 58 degrees Fahrenheit [ 14.4 Celsius ] is rather cool in the heat of a 45 Celsius summer, and rather warm compared to a -10 Celsius winter, and therefore, if all it does is keep people in the SAFE zone, ESPECIALLY if one lives any place where the climate reaches either extreme [say a desert on the one hand, although free flowing water may not be as readily available there, some places it is, and in a jungle its pretty available] or an area quite close to the planet's poles, it will potentially save one a fortune on primary heating.

From there, though, one COULD simply add a SMALLER, INTERNAL space, with some additional insulation, so that they are in effect heating or cooling just a little living space, inside of a larger buffer area, so that it will cost only the loss or gain of heat from a roughly 14 degree Celsius environment.

This approach is not 'utterly foolproof' of course, because water, in this situation, is highly dependent on nature and the surrounding availability for one to obtain it.

For instance, if one went to an outside flowing creek, there may indeed be a lot of water, but it will have been somewhat altered by the surface temperature before it gets to you, so it may be cooler, or warmer, than the 14 C middle-of-the-road temperature. And if the water is coming from the earth, but it is from a hot spring, while it will be much better for heating, it may not be so good for cooling, nor anything else, as it may be packed with both dissolved minerals that muck up the system, as well as dangerous gases like hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide, and may be highly acidic with sulfuric acid. Then, going back to the desert scenario, there just may not be a whole lot of ground water readily available near the surface of the earth in those regions, in which case, you might be better off simply digging a large cavern and living in the cooler underground completely...or moving to some better location altogether.

But for much of the planet, the possibility that you live near a hill, with some water in it, is statistically about 25% or so, and if you do not live up against a decent sized hill, BUT you have a well, you can simply recirculate your well water with some additional pipe and a pump, for the heat, although it will then of course cost you the electricity to pump the water up to the radiator and run a fan, because gravity can not in that situation be relied upon to make the geothermal mass of the water flow and do the work for you.

author: @gorgosanma
text:
Maybe I'm a bit weird, but hear me out. Instead of heaters I spend more money in light clothing that keeps me warm and at the same time comfortable. During the winter when I go outside I rarely use anything more than a shirt and a jacket because I choose the materials. When I'm indoors I get more choices because there's no wind, that's when wool can be the outer layer. 
It sure helps to have heating in my place, but it is much more efficient if I'm not letting my body heat just go so easily.
By the way, I love your channel. I found it a week ago and it's motivating me to go back to my "making things" mindset.

author: @davidsoulsby1102
text:
Kanthal A1 wire Iron, Chromium and Aluminum, another heating wire, slightly higher resistance but more robust and cheaper than NiChrome.
Widely used in domestic heaters due to its longer life expectancy which is due to its steady "elasticity" over a greater temperature range.
Nichrome is cleaner and lower resistance per meter but shorter life, used more for high end and shorter duration. If your building portable go for Kanthal it can be moved while still white hot but nichrome could snap.
Note not all Kanthal is Kanthal, its a specific patented brand name with circa100 year history, now sold under Sandvik Kathal name. if its not from them its no true kanthal and may be lower quality.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice advice - cheers mate

author: @dannyboy9817
text: As some other viewers have commented, heat pumps are of course a viable alternative to resistance heating as they work by collecting and concentrating low temperature heat from elsewhere into higher temperature heat where you need it. This technology has been around for decades but is an expensive investment. Those mis-advertised little plug in mini heaters that claim to heat your room for a fraction of the cost of a "normal" heater are utter cons, underpinned by utter lies, aimed at folks who aren't technically informed. It's scandalous and disgusts me. Rant over. Your video is spot on Robert. If you could bring yourself to foot the purchase price of one of those ridiculous things, it might make the basis of a cracking video to demonstrate that they don't actually do what they claim.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree with both things you are saying mate - I am planning a heat pump series and yes those heaters are pure scam - I will buy one I think

author: @tobygathergood4990
text: The way I have found to make it less damp, and therefore somewhat warmer in my house is to have a dehumidifier running constantly. I tip out 15L of water from it's collector about every 36 hours. This way, whatever heat source  am using, I'm only heating up the air, but not the moisture content of the air as well.

replies.author: @NefariouZ32
replies.text: this makes a surprising difference. we do this as well, and have noticed we can even be comfortable with a lower indoor air temperature (1-2c lower) with the lower humidity air, compared to the higher humidity environment. We also live in a humid area in winter, and going from 70-80% indoor humidity to ~50% humidity makes a huge difference to the "feeling" of the space, regardless of the actual temperature. we are also starting the process of installing heat pumps to the space to further reduce electrical usage

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: why don't you just use calcium chloride?

replies.author: @tobygathergood4990
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering The cost of 50lbs of CaCl2 is $36 cdn. It melts when collecting H2O, so I don't want to have to deal with the resulting liquid and having to constantly replace it. CaCl2 is, as you know, exothermic. I just don't want to have to deal with all of that. A dehumidifier can be expensive, but I was fortunate enough to find a new small industrial unit for very cheap, so I went with that. It will gather the 15L or H2O in the 36 hr. period from a 1,200+ sq ft area. I do have however cheap small CaCl2 moisture catchers that hold about a pint each in my unheated workshop to keep my more vulnerable tools from flash rust. I do have a propane forced air heater I picked up some years ago from a scrapper, that I need to refurbish to that end as well. Unfortunately the current temps outside are nearing 0 Fahrenheit. So not going to do that either until warmer...much warmer...one day.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Great explanation. Please don't forget that heat pumps can make heating / cooling more efficient. Specifically... if you need 1000 watts to heat a room using 100% resistance heat, then a heat pump would require "less" than that... provided that the external environment hadn't dropped below some specific cutoff, such as the freezing point.

author: @ryanbrown982
text: You make heating more efficient by using a heat pump. Those can reach 3-500% efficient because you aren't creating heat, but *moving* it. But that's a lot more complicated than just shorting out a wire. And you have to have somewhere to move the heat from, which means either a window unit or a permanent installation. Ideal for a long term setup, but not so much when you're just trying to stay warm for the winter due to geopolitical unpleasantness.

replies.author: @ricos1497
replies.text: I don't think you have to give them back after the unpleasantness subsides in fairness.

replies.author: @ryanbrown982
replies.text: @ricos1497 Sure, but if there is suddenly a huge demand for heat pumps and heat pump installers, it can be difficult and expensive to get the equipment and have it installed in time for this winter. Wire and a 12v supply is fast and dirt cheap comparatively.

replies.author: @ricos1497
replies.text: @ryanbrown982 I was just being facetious. I got you. You're entirely correct.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yep and they are a bit more complicated than quite a few folks can handle

replies.author: @CallMeMrChainmail
replies.text: The problem with heat pumps is that because the UK doesn't really have an AC industry we don't really have installers. The govt. has been trying to change that HOWEVER, the British govt. is absolutely terrified that British people might use AC in the summer and has subsequently taken steps in opposition to certain types of systems and installer training. That means that the systems that we do have and that are being installed have frequently turned out to be colossal failures. You can google the complaints yourself. Heat pumps and their installers have developed a very bad reputation in a very short space of time.

author: @williambianchi2006
text:
This is fascinating and great to know, but it works only if the electricity is available via power company, generator, or batteries. Old fashioned flames are more flexible, in my opinion. Rocket stoves, propane/butane camp stoves and barbeque grills, candles, charcoal grills and alcohol stoves work well during power outages.
Just my opinion based on what I think is coming this winter. I could easily be wrong, so prepare according to your circumstances in your location.

replies.author: @xXTheoLinuxXx
replies.text: I don't have experience with those heaters, but I know a few things about them.  3KW isn't exactly a lot in most cases. You need to know how well that room is insolated and the ammount of m3 that place is. I can give you an example of my living room which is 5.60 x 3.80 and around 2.50 high. It is insolated and has double glass.  When I do the math (on a Dutch site) it tells me that I need almost 4kw to get it to 20 degrees celcius. I was thinking about buying a similar heater too, but in my case I go for a larger one (5kw should be enough).

replies.author: @helencrystals1111
replies.text: @xXTheoLinuxXx I would get bigger, but unfortunately the British version only comes in 3kw, probably to discourage us from using them to heat our houses, that being said, I'm in a very small well insulated bungalow, so I think it is still possible it could be effective enough to be worthwhile.
